Toss Update: RR won the toss and decided to bat first! 

X FACTOR EXUBERANCE 

Sai Sudharsan has quietly established himself as a silk smooth X factor at the top and through the middle, a left hander whose elegance is matched by ruthless consistency. In 41 matches, he’s piled up an impressive 1,806 runs. He is the very definition of silky consistency and scoreboard pressure. The left hander has quietly grown into a bankable game changer, blending classical strokeplay with modern acceleration. Across 41 matches, Sudarshan has piled up 1,806 runs at an outstanding average of 48.81, striking at a healthy 145.65. Those numbers scream reliability with intent — the kind of batter who anchors an innings yet shifts gears seamlessly when the moment demands.

Vaibhav Sooryavanshi is a raw talent turned electric impact — a fearless U19 prodigy already announcing himself on the big stage. Also a left hander, Vaibhav plays with breathtaking freedom, taking bowlers on from ball one. In just 8 matches, he has smashed 304 runs at an imposing average of 38, but it’s his jaw?dropping strike rate of 218.71 that truly sets him apart. Blink and you miss it — that’s the tempo he brings. Youthful audacity meets clean hitting, and despite his age, Vaibhav has shown he doesn’t just belong here — he can redefine momentum in a matter of overs.

 

PLAYER BATTLES TO WATCH OUT FOR 

Shubman Gill vs Jofra Archer
This is a mouth watering contest right at the top of the innings. Shubman Gill, the elegant right hand opening batter, thrives on rhythm and timing, but Jofra Archer brings sheer pace, bounce, and hostility with the new ball. The numbers highlight how fiercely contested this duel has been: Gill has faced 19 deliveries, managing just 16 runs, while Archer has struck 3 times, regularly testing Gill with high pace and hard lengths. In broadcast terms, it’s classic power versus poise — Archer hunting early breakthroughs with raw speed, and Gill looking to weather the storm before unfurling his trademark drives once set.

Shimron Hetmyer vs Rashid Khan
At the death or in the middle overs, this matchup crackles with intent. Shimron Hetmyer, the explosive left handed finisher, loves taking on spin, but Rashid Khan’s world class leg spin has often had the final say. Across 81 balls, Hetmyer has smashed 108 runs, showing his ability to dominate, yet Rashid’s cunning and control are underscored by 7 dismissals — a reminder of how often he has outfoxed the Caribbean dasher. It’s muscle versus mystery: Hetmyer looking to clear the ropes under pressure, and Rashid varying pace and trajectory, betting on skill and deception to turn the game on its head.

VENUE AT A GLANCE

 

MATCH PREVIEW

Match number nine of the TATA IPL 2026 brings the spotlight to Ahmedabad as the Narendra Modi Stadium lights up for the first time this season, with Gujarat Titans squaring off against Rajasthan Royals in a clash rich with recent history. These were the finalists of the 2022 edition, and while circumstances are different now, the edge and expectation remain firmly intact.

Gujarat Titans walk into this contest under pressure, still searching for their first points of the season. Their previous outing against Punjab Kings exposed a fragile middle order, as GT failed to capitalise on promising starts from the top three and were restricted to a sub 170 total in a tight defeat. One shining positive, however, was Prasidh Krishna, whose fiery three wicket burst nearly turned the game on its head. GT will be desperate for a more complete performance tonight as they look to get their campaign up and running.

Rajasthan Royals, in contrast, arrive with confidence flowing. They opened their season with an emphatic eight wicket victory, dismantling Chennai Super Kings with ruthless efficiency. Their bowlers set the tone early, applying relentless pressure, before the chase was lit up by Sooryavanshi, who produced a breathtaking 15 ball fifty, the fastest of IPL 2026 so far. It was a statement win that underlined both intent and depth.

Momentum favours the Royals, urgency fuels the Titans. Ahmedabad promises pace, bounce and big moments, with both sides knowing how quickly seasons can swing. As memories of 2022 resurface and the stakes rise under the lights, the question echoes through the stands. Who takes control tonight, desperation or dominance?
MatchSummary.PostMatchCommentary:
Ravi Bishnoi (Player of the Match):
"It was a tough season last year and I realised whenever I missed my length I was getting punished, so I focused on tightening that area and accepting the result if batters still hit me. It took mental, technical and physical effort together and I bowled a lot of deliveries to keep improving. My favourite wicket was Rahul Tewatia because he is known to change games in such situations and his dismissal was crucial. If he had stayed, the match could have gone either way. The death bowling from Tushar and Jofra showed how collective the effort was, and as a young team we want to keep entertaining."
 

Riyan Parag (Rajasthan Royals Captain):
"The last two overs were simply incredible and taking the call to bowl full and fast paid off because the boys executed exactly what was planned. I was extremely excited watching them deliver under pressure. I was close to changing the order but Jurel backed Jofra for the nineteenth and that turned out to be a great call. The idea was to bowl as quick and straight as possible at the end and the execution was brilliant. Jurel has done the hard work batting lower down and now at three he has the chance to score big and hopefully lead us to the title."


Rashid Khan (Gujarat Titans Captain):

"We bowled really well overall, especially after the strong start they made, and with the bat too we began nicely before losing back-to-back wickets, which pushed us on the back foot. That phase cost us momentum and on a surface like this scoring nine or ten an over is manageable if you keep the boundaries coming. Losing quick wickets in the middle did not allow us to finish the innings the way we wanted. Everyone has their own game plan but reading conditions and match requirements is crucial and it is still early in the tournament so we will learn and come back stronger. In the final over the talk was about finding one boundary, Tushar executed his yorkers well and I should have taken the chance when he missed, we stay calm, learn from this and hopefully Shubman will be fine for the next game."


Rajasthan Royals held their nerve in a high-scoring encounter at Ahmedabad, completing a tight six-run victory over Gujarat Titans after both sides traded punches through all forty overs in a contest defined by sustained scoring and late tension.

Batting first, Rajasthan posted an imposing 210 for 6, built around a fluent 75 from Dhruv Jurel off 42 balls at a strike rate nearing 180. Yashasvi Jaiswal set the base with 55 from 36 deliveries, putting on 126 for the first two wickets as Rajasthan reached the halfway mark at 113 for 1. Support arrived in bursts, with Shimron Hetmyer striking 18 off 8 and the Royals scoring at over ten an over in 14 of the 20 overs. Gujarat’s bowlers struggled for control, conceding 13 wides, with Rabada finishing with 2 for 42.

Gujarat’s reply stayed competitive throughout. Sai Sudharsan anchored the chase superbly with 73 from 44 balls, bringing up his fifty in just 33 deliveries as Gujarat crossed 100 by the tenth over. A sharp collapse followed as Ravi Bishnoi ripped through the middle order with figures of 4 for 41, removing four batters between overs 12 and 15 to swing momentum Rajasthan’s way. Rashid Khan and Kagiso Rabada mounted a late surge, adding 47 in the final five overs, but the ask proved just beyond reach.

Gujarat closed on 204 for 8, falling short despite scoring at over ten an over in 11 overs, as Rajasthan’s middle overs control proved decisive in a thriller decided by fine margins.
